specification.topic-statement: The tool execution pipeline dispatches an LLM-requested tool call to either an in-process handler or an external executable, wraps the result, and appends both the call and the result to the conversation before continuing the LLM request loop.

scope.in-scope: Trigger conditions, the two execution paths (internal and external), the worker thread lifecycle, completion polling, result wrapping, URI translation, database persistence, the tool registry and discovery, and the tool loop continuation mechanism.
scope.boundary: How the LLM API request is formed and sent, and how the streaming response is parsed to detect tool calls, is covered by the provider and SSE parser specifications.
The state transitions between idle, waiting-for-LLM, and executing-tool are covered by the agent state machine specification.

field.name: name
field.note: the tool's callable name (e.g., 'bash', 'file_read'); derived from the executable filename by stripping the '-tool' suffix and converting hyphens to underscores

field.name: path
field.note: absolute filesystem path to the executable; null for internal tools

field.name: schema
field.note: the tool's JSON schema, obtained by invoking the tool with the '--schema' flag during discovery

field.name: handler
field.note: the C function for internal tools; null for external tools

field.name: on-complete hook
field.note: called on the main thread after the tool's worker thread finishes; null if no post-completion work is needed

field.name: tool_success
field.note: true if the tool executed without error

field.name: result
field.note: present when tool_success is true; the tool's output

field.name: error
field.note: present when tool_success is false; a human-readable error message

field.name: error_code
field.note: present when tool_success is false; a short machine-readable error identifier

behavior.trigger:
Tool execution begins when an LLM response is received with a tool call content block and a finish reason indicating tool use. The tool call's name, identifier, and JSON arguments are extracted from the response and stored as the agent's pending tool call.
Only the first tool call block in a response is executed. Any additional tool call blocks in the same response are discarded.
behavior.worker-thread:
Tool execution runs on a dedicated worker thread, not on the main event loop thread. The thread is created when execution begins and joined when the main loop detects completion.
While the thread is running, the agent is in the executing-tool state. The main loop polls for completion every 50 milliseconds by checking an atomic completion flag.
When the flag is set, the main loop joins the thread, harvests the result from a shared field, and proceeds with result processing.
There is no asynchronous notification. The main loop polls the completion flag on every iteration when any agent is in the executing-tool state and joins the thread synchronously when the flag is observed as set.
behavior.internal-tool-path:
If the tool name matches a registered internal tool, its handler function is called directly on the worker thread. The handler receives a talloc allocator context, the agent context, and the JSON arguments string. It returns a JSON string result.
Internal tools currently registered are: - **fork**: creates a child agent with a given name and prompt. - **kill**: terminates a named agent and all of its descendants. - **send**: delivers a message to another agent. - **wait**: blocks until a message is received from one or more other agents.
behavior.external-tool-path:
If the tool name matches a registered external tool, the tool executable is invoked as a child process.
Before invocation, `ik://` URIs in the JSON arguments are translated to absolute filesystem paths. After the tool returns, any absolute filesystem paths in the result that fall under the state or data directories are translated back to `ik://` URIs. This translation is transparent to the tool process.
The executable is launched as a child process with: - The JSON arguments supplied on standard input. - The calling agent's UUID set in the `IKIGAI_AGENT_ID` environment variable. - A new process group (the child becomes its own process group leader), enabling group-based interruption of any subprocesses it spawns. - Standard output and standard error captured via pipes.
A 30-second alarm is set before reading from the pipes. If the tool has not produced all output within 30 seconds, the alarm fires.
The parent reads all of the child's standard output and standard error via the pipes, then waits for the child to exit. If the child exits with a non-zero status, the standard error output (if any) is returned as an error. If the child produces no standard output, an error is returned. If the child produces standard output and exits cleanly, the output is the tool result.
behavior.result-wrapping:
All tool results — from both internal and external paths — are wrapped in a standard envelope before being added to the conversation. A successful result becomes `{"tool_success": true, "result": }`. A failure becomes `{"tool_success": false, "error": "", "error_code": ""}`.
Invalid JSON returned by an external tool is treated as a failure with the error code `INVALID_OUTPUT`.
A lookup failure (the tool name is not in the registry) is treated as a failure with the error code `tool_not_found`.
behavior.conversation-update:
After the result is collected and wrapped, two messages are added to the conversation:
1. A tool call message (assistant role) containing the original tool call block and any thinking blocks from the LLM response. 2. A tool result message (tool role) containing the tool call identifier and the wrapped result.
Both messages are persisted to the database and rendered to the scrollback.
behavior.on-complete-hook: If the tool registry entry has an on-complete hook, it is called on the main thread after the result has been harvested from the worker thread. The hook may modify REPL state (for example, the fork tool's hook transfers the newly created child agent into the REPL's agent array). The hook runs before the tool loop continuation.
behavior.tool-loop-continuation:
After result processing, the agent automatically resubmits the conversation to the LLM. The LLM may respond with another tool call, continuing the loop, or with a text response that ends the loop. Each tool call iteration increments an iteration counter on the agent.
There is no hardcoded or configured maximum number of tool loop iterations. The loop continues as long as the LLM requests tool calls. The iteration counter is tracked but not checked against a limit.
behavior.tool-discovery:
The tool registry is populated at startup and on `/refresh` by scanning three directories in order: the system libexec directory, the user tools directory (`~/.ikigai/tools/`), and the project tools directory (`.ikigai/tools/` relative to the current working directory). Tools found in later directories override tools with the same name found in earlier directories.
An executable file qualifies as a tool if its name ends with `-tool`. The tool name is derived by stripping the `-tool` suffix and replacing hyphens with underscores in the remainder.
Each candidate tool is invoked with the `--schema` flag and given 1 second to output its JSON schema on standard output. If the invocation times out, produces no output, or produces invalid JSON, the tool is skipped and not added to the registry.
Internal tools are registered unconditionally after external tools are discovered.
